text: The basicity order of amines in aqueous solution is:
explanation: In aqueous solution (alkyl amines): 2° > 1° > 3° > NH₃. 3° has steric hindrance reducing solvation of the conjugate acid. 2° has best balance.
name: Basicity Order of Amines

text: Gabriel phthalimide synthesis is used to prepare:
explanation: Gabriel synthesis: phthalimide + KOH → potassium phthalimide + RX → N-alkyl phthalimide + N₂H₄/H₂O → pure 1° amine. No 2°/3° contamination.
name: Gabriel Synthesis

text: Hofmann bromamide degradation converts amide to:
explanation: Hofmann rearrangement: RCONH₂ + Br₂ + KOH → RNH₂ (1° amine) + CO₂. Product has one fewer C than reactant. Migrates R group to nitrogen.
name: Hofmann Bromamide Reaction

text: The reaction of primary aromatic amine with NaNO₂/HCl at 0–5°C gives:
explanation: Diazotisation: ArNH₂ + NaNO₂ + HCl (0–5°C) → ArN₂⁺Cl⁻ (diazonium salt). Must be at low temp (diazonium salts are unstable).
name: Diazotisation

text: Coupling reaction of diazonium salt with phenol/aniline gives:
explanation: Diazonium coupling: ArN₂⁺ + PhOH (or PhNH₂) → Ar–N=N–Ph (azo compound). Electrophilic aromatic substitution at activated aromatic ring.
name: Diazonium Coupling

text: Sandmeyer reaction converts diazonium salt to aryl halide using:
explanation: Sandmeyer: ArN₂⁺ + CuCl → ArCl + N₂. ArN₂⁺ + CuBr → ArBr + N₂. ArN₂⁺ + CuCN → ArCN + N₂. Replaces N₂ with X or CN.
name: Sandmeyer Reaction

text: Carbylamine reaction (isocyanide test) is given by:
options: Primary amines (1°) to give foul-smelling isocyanide
explanation: Carbylamine test: 1° amine + CHCl₃ + KOH → isocyanide (R–NC), very unpleasant smell. Positive only for primary amines. Test for 1° amines.
name: Carbylamine Test

text: The IUPAC name of C₂H₅NC is:
explanation: R–N≡C: isocyanide (R–NC or R–isocyanide). C₂H₅NC = ethyl isocyanide. C₂H₅CN = ethyl cyanide = propanenitrile (IUPAC: propanenitrile).
name: Isocyanide Nomenclature

text: Amines react with acyl chloride (RCOCl) to give:
explanation: R'NH₂ + RCOCl → RCONHR' + HCl. Nucleophilic acyl substitution. Acylation of amine (Schotten-Baumann conditions).
name: Acylation of Amines

text: In the reaction of aliphatic diazonium compounds (R–N₂⁺), the diazonium group is lost as:
explanation: Aliphatic diazonium ions are very unstable (even at 0°C): R–N₂⁺ → R⁺ + N₂↑. The loss of N₂ drives the reaction forward.
name: Aliphatic Diazonium Decomposition

text: Reduction of a nitrile (R–CN) with LiAlH₄ gives:
explanation: R–CN + LiAlH₄ → R–CH₂–NH₂ (primary amine). The nitrile carbon is reduced to –CH₂–. Useful synthetic route.
name: Reduction of Nitrile

text: The Gattermann reaction is a modification of Sandmeyer reaction using:
options: Cu powder (instead of CuX salt) + HX gas
explanation: Gattermann reaction: ArN₂⁺ + Cu powder + HX → ArX + N₂. Uses Cu metal + HX instead of CuX salt (Sandmeyer).
name: Gattermann Reaction

text: Acetylation of aniline (PhNH₂) with acetic anhydride gives:
explanation: PhNH₂ + (CH₃CO)₂O → PhNH–COCH₃ (acetanilide) + CH₃COOH. Protects amino group from oxidation during synthesis.
name: Acetylation of Aniline

text: Hinsberg test distinguishes between 1°, 2°, and 3° amines using:
explanation: Hinsberg test: 1° amine + PhSO₂Cl → N-alkylbenzenesulfonamide (soluble in NaOH). 2° → insoluble sulfonamide. 3° → no reaction.
name: Hinsberg Test

text: The basicity of aniline (PhNH₂) is much less than aliphatic amines because:
options: Lone pair on N is delocalised into the aromatic ring (resonance), making it less available
N has no lone pair in aniline
explanation: In aniline: N lone pair delocalised into aromatic π system via resonance → less available for protonation. pKb(aniline) ≈ 9.4 vs pKb(CH₃NH₂) ≈ 3.4.
name: Aniline Low Basicity
